text: met her,’ ‘But you know Dr Ketterley?’ ‘I ... Yes. I knew him briefly. It was .... Oh God, when was it? A few years ago now.’ Pause. ‘Um, is this about ... 7’ ‘About ... ?’ ‘Well ... Now that you’ve mentioned Dr Ketterley’s ex-wife ...’ ‘Dr Hubert.’ ‘Yes. I know that a while back Dr Hubert was going around telling people that Val was stalking me. Is it about that?’ Pause. ‘Well, yes. I suppose so. A couple of people we’ve spoken to have said that they believed Dr Ketterley may have stalked you or behaved in a way that might be interpreted as stalking. So what we want to do nowis ... ’ ‘It isn’t true.’ ‘Oh! Right. OK. Good to know.’ ‘I never got the least sign from him that he was interested in me in that way. If anything I thought he was rather bored.’ ‘OK. Well, perhaps you could say something about how you knew him. I’m interested to know why these other people thought he was stalking you. How did you meet?’ ‘Through a mutual friend. Liz. We were both at a dinner party at Liz’s house, seated next to each other. And we had the usual sort of conversation. How do you know Liz? — what do you do? — what sort of interests do you have? That sort of thing. As I say he didn’t exactly seem riveted by my company. But afterwards he called me and said he wanted to commission me to do some work.’ ‘And did you do it? The work? Sorry, I don’t actually know what it is that you do,’ ‘I’m a cartographer. I make maps.’ ‘Oh, really! That sounds interesting’ ‘It is. Very.’ ‘So ... ? Dr Ketterley wanted you to make a map?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘Of what?’ ‘He didn’t say. Not at the beginning. He told me that he wanted me to go with him to some unspecified place and then I could make a map of it.’ ‘OK. Well, I’m beginning to see why people thought he was stalking you. What did you say?’ ‘I can’t really remember. | think I was a bit flummoxed. It’s not what I do. When I make maps I use data that someone else has provided. The data might be geographical features, street grids, whatever’s required. I don’t do any initial surveying. There are all sorts of easily accessible apps and databases and a lot of the data is free. I think I explained all this and then I said, “Just tell me where the place is and what sort of map you want and I'll find the data.” ‘And what did he say?’ ‘There was a long silence. And then he said, “There is no data ‘Could he have been talking about somewhere remote?’ ”) ‘No. It was near London. He said we could be there and back in a day.’ ‘OK. Let me see if I’ve understood. He wanted you to make a map of somewhere that was less than a day’s travel from London, but at the same time he was claiming there were no maps of this place at all?’ ‘Exactly. It made no sense. I wanted to end the call at that point, but he switched tactics and said we should meet up and then he’d be able to explain better. So we made an arrange- ment to have dinner at a place near the Barbican. I was quite nervous because the whole thing seemed so peculiar, but I went anyway. And when I got there he told me it was a maze. He wanted me to make a map of a maze. So as far as I was concerned that threw the whole project into a different light. I’ve always loved mazes and labyrinths. You can make such beautiful maps of them. Straightaway I was on board. I asked if he was sure that a plan of the maze didn’t exist somewhere. He said that it was possible that someone had made a map in the past, but he had no way of accessing it. He said he’d tried making a map of it himself, but it hadn’t worked. The layout was too complicated; he couldn’t get his head round it. I asked him if he’d discovered the way through it. And he said it wasn’t about finding a way through, it was about what’s inside.’ ‘And what was that?’ ‘Statues, he said. And possibly other things. He said there were parts of it he hadn’t explored. I asked him if anyone else would be there. And he said no, it would be completely deserted. So then we starting making plans for our trip, and he said...’ ‘Hold on a minute. By this time he’d told you where it was?’ ‘No.’ “You still didn’t know where it was?’ ‘No, but that didn’t bother me any more. You see I’d worked out what was going on. This place — wherever it was — it must have been like Burnt Norton.’ ‘Like Burnt Norton?’ ‘Yes.’ ‘Sorry. I’ve no idea what that means.’ ‘Burnt Norton was a garden. I mean, it still is I suppose. T. S. Eliot went there and he wrote a poem about it. But he wasn’t supposed to be there. He didn’t have permission or anything. He just trespassed. And then he produced an extraordinary poem. And I think something similar happened to Val. He’d discovered a hedge maze in an old garden — the garden of a derelict house where no one ever went. I think he must have asked the owners of the garden if he could examine it and document it and they’d said no. But he’d gone anyway. That was why he didn’t want to say where it was. In case the owners found out he was going there.’ AS) ee ‘After our meeting at the restaurant I thought a lot about it. I could see it so clearly in my mind. The maze and the deserted
